findings.quote: Building a clock that kept accurate time under conditions at sea.
findings.note: Harrison's marine-chronometer story is common knowledge: the obstacle was a sea-worthy timekeeper. But A/C are substantive alternative obstacles and D a real competing method; the exact key needs the passage.

findings.quote: Techniques that maximise engagement draw on the psychology of the slot machine.
findings.note: Variable-reward / slot-machine framing is the well-known attention-economy claim (Harris). Other options are substantive statements; a passage is needed to confirm which it supports.

findings.quote: On a conference in 2014, several researchers presented informations that questioned
findings.note: genre & source-plausibility: 'On a conference' and the uncountable-as-plural 'informations' are non-native constructions a real New Scientist copy desk would never print, denting the source-faithfulness the ELF register requires; genre and temperature are otherwise correct.

findings.quote: Eventually the wolves will be blamed for less than the first enthusiastic reports suggested.
findings.note: register: the future-tense framing of a past scholarly revision reads oddly for magazine science journalism; contrast the crisp past-tense reporting in var-2023-verb2-ELF-039.

findings.quote: Factors other than wolves may explain much of the recovery.
findings.note: Recent scholarship qualifies the Yellowstone cascade by crediting other factors. Distractors are substantive (B opposite, C/D specific claims); passage needed to decide.

findings.quote: Let's be honest — you probably can't function without your morning coffee, and you're definitely not alone!
findings.note: class 4: register break — clickbait/listicle voice with contractions, direct reader address and exclamation; not the serious-magazine expository register the ELF section imitates. Editor's rewrite recasts in third-person expository prose.

findings.quote: Ready to have your mind blown? Here are five amazing facts that will make you love your daily cup even more.
findings.note: class 4: marketing cadence and direct reader address out of keeping with academic-magazine genre.

findings.quote: And here's the kicker — that "coffee" buzz isn't just in your head.
findings.note: class 4: sudden informality ('here's the kicker') and colloquialism inappropriate to the target register.

findings.quote: Pretty wild, huh?
findings.note: class 4: chatty tag question; house-genre mismatch.

findings.quote: Let's be honest — you probably can't function without your morning coffee, and you're definitely not alone! Coffee is one of the most popular drinks on the planet
findings.note: class 4: register break — clickbait/listicle cadence with contractions, exclamation, and direct second-person address, wholly outside the serious academic-magazine ELF register. Grammatically native but wrong genre.

findings.quote: Ready to have your mind blown? Here are five amazing facts that will make you love your daily cup even more.
findings.note: class 4: marketing cadence and reader-address ('Ready to have your mind blown?', 'the kicker', 'How cool is that?') an editor at an academic-adjacent publication would strike entirely.

findings.quote: you're basically drinking science. How cool is that?
findings.note: class 4: sudden informality and rhetorical reader-address; no concrete anchors, named sources, or hedged claims characteristic of authentic ELF texture.

findings.quote: Let’s be honest — you probably can’t function without your morning coffee, and you’re definitely not alone!
findings.note: class 4: sustained clickbait/listicle register with direct reader address, contractions and exclamation — wholly outside the serious-magazine expository register ELF sources imitate. Grammatical native English, but the wrong genre throughout.

findings.quote: Ready to have your mind blown? Here are five amazing facts that will make you love your daily cup even more.
findings.note: class 4: marketing cadence and hype ("mind blown", "amazing facts") an academic-magazine editor would strike; register break, not an ESL error.

findings.quote: Pretty wild, huh?
findings.note: class 4: colloquial direct address out of keeping with the genre; reinforces the register mismatch. Rewrite: delete or recast as expository prose.

findings.quote: It was originally eaten rather than drunk.
findings.note: C and D are origin/history facts that do not answer the stem's mechanism question ('why more awake'), so they can be dropped blind as off-topic; that still leaves A (adenosine block) vs B undecided without the passage.

findings.quote: The arguments lost their force since women proved to be the best wartime diplomats of all.
findings.note: C ('completely changed') and D ('best wartime diplomats of all') are overstated absolutes eliminable blind, leaving the moderate A/B pair; passage needed to decide between them.

findings.quote: Virtually nothing of the Pantheon erected by Agrippa remains today.
findings.note: World knowledge of the Pantheon lets one eliminate B and lean to A, but the exact key is not decided blind; distractors substantive (calibration example).

findings.quote: delprov elf engelsk läsförståelse what is the main
findings.note: 8-gram containment 0.0115 exceeds flag threshold 0.01 (3/260 grams shared)

findings.quote: Kontroll över vardagens rytm och därmed över människorna.
findings.note: 'Ytterst handlade om' pairs with the single abstract option A while B/C/D are concrete specifics, and standardisation-as-control is a known theme; can eliminate the concretes but passage needed to confirm.

findings.quote: Många arter är känsliga för föroreningar som svaveldioxid.
findings.note: Lichens as SO2 bioindicators is domain knowledge pointing to A; B/C/D are substantive (a wrong mechanism and off-topic uses), so passage still grounds the answer.

findings.quote: Okej, så här är grejen med gamla kartor: de är egentligen ganska sjukt opålitliga.
findings.note: class 5: register break — colloquial talspråk ('okej', 'grejen', 'sjukt') in a text that must be edited sakprosa.

findings.quote: Forskaren Chet Van Duzer, som har kollat på jättemånga sådana kartor
findings.note: class 5: colloquialisms 'kollat på' and 'jättemånga' are off-register for exam sakprosa.

findings.quote: Kanske borde vi återinföra monstren, typ som en påminnelse om att vi inte kan allt.
findings.note: class 5: filler 'typ' plus direct reader address elsewhere ('nästa gång du zoomar in') sustain a blog/tal register no authentic HP LÄS text would use.

findings.quote: De markerade gränsen mot det farliga och okända.
findings.note: Chet Van Duzer is a real map historian whose thesis (Sea Monsters on Medieval Maps) is that monsters marked the dangerous/unknown, not mere decoration. B is the opposing scholarly position he argues against — substantive, not filler (Ostrom-type case) → flag.

findings.quote: Att tystnaden är en historiskt skapad norm snarare än något givet.
findings.note: That library silence is a historical construction (reading aloud was once normal) is domain knowledge favouring A; B is the substantive opposite claim, so a passage grounds it.

findings.quote: Att gemensam förvaltning kan fungera hållbart med rätt institutioner.
findings.note: This is Elinor Ostrom's Nobel thesis against Hardin. B/D are opposite substantive stances in a live scholarly debate, not filler; key matching published scholarship = world knowledge → flag (calibration hard negative).

findings.quote: Uppfunna traditioner uppstår ofta i tider av snabb förändring.
findings.note: Hobsbawm's 'invention of tradition' thesis links invented traditions to periods of rapid change; distractors are substantive (a generic claim, a datable factual claim, an aim-mischaracterisation) so passage grounds it.

findings.quote: På grund av dåtidens tillverkningsmetod.
findings.note: Domain knowledge (the 'glass flows' claim is a myth; thickness is from period manufacture) favours A; B and C are the popular misconception as substantive distractors, not structural filler → flag not recall.

findings.quote: Att felaktigheter består.
findings.note: The 'rundgång' (feedback-loop) metaphor implies recirculation/persistence, letting one drop A ('samband försvinner') and D ('forskningen missförstås') as off-metaphor; B vs C still needs the passage.

findings.quote: Att gränsen mellan vad som ska betraktas som botbara respektive obotliga sjukdomar förflyttas.
findings.note: ARGUABLE: Norlén's own definition is 'inte antalet patienter, utan att man glider på indikationerna'. Key C (habituation/tillvänjning shifting right-and-wrong) is the intended general reading, but a well-prepared examinee could read 'glider på indikationerna' as the curable/incurable boundary moving (D), invoking the earlier note that indications widened from terminal illness to psychiatric diagnoses. Attractive but beatable: it imports a different commenter's elaboration and narrows Norlén's general point to one axis.

findings.quote: Att det sker en tillvänjningsprocess som förskjuter tidigare uppfattningar om rätt och fel.
findings.note: 'Sluttande plan' (slippery slope) is a gradual-normalisation metaphor; A and B (about decision authority) are eliminable blind, leaving C vs D (both shifts) undecided without the passage.

findings.quote: Etniskt splittrade samhällen.
findings.note: The claimed 'cluster' (which factors argue for vs against an uprising) is only visible by reasoning about the domain — from form alone the four options are parallel; domain reasoning in a structural costume → flag, not kill (calibration authentic item).
